coredata.dc:title: Highly selective fluorescence probe for imidacloprid measurement based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Jinmei
Li, Shuhuai
Pang, Chaohai
Wang, Mingyue
Ma, Xionghui
Zhang, Chenghui
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a fluorescent probe was investigated for the detection of imidacloprid based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between two carbon-based quantum dots, namely graphene- and carbon quantum dots (GQDs/C-dots). In an HAc-NaAc buffer solution at pH = 5.0, an effective FRET occurred between the GQDs and C-dots, significantly enhancing the C-dot fluorescence. When imidacloprid was added to the FRET system, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots was quenched in a linear imidacloprid concentration-dependent manner, which allowed for the establishment of a new method for the determination of imidacloprid by GQD/C-dot FRET. Under optimised experimental conditions, the linear range of imidacloprid was 5–4000 × 10−9 mol L−1, with a correlation coefficient r and a detection limit of 0.998 and 8.23 × 10−10 mol L−1, respectively. This method was applied to the detection of imidacloprid residues in real samples, and a satisfactory recovery rate of standard additions was found between 93.0% and 105.6%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Carbon quantum dot
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Quenching
Imidacloprid

Study on the FRET between GQDs and C-dots
Study on determination of imidacloprid by GQDs/C-dots energy transfer system
Study on the GQDs/C-dots energy transfer system
Study on the fluorescence parameters of the GQDs/C-dots energy transfer system
Study on the quenching mechanism of imidacloprid and the fluorescence of the GQDs/C-dots system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the GQDs/C-dots system for imidacloprid detection by FRET.
(A) TEM images, (B) particle size distribution, and (C) UV light irradiation diagram of GQDs. (D) TEM images, (E) particle size distribution, and (F) UV light irradiation diagram of C-dots.

(A) Emission spectrum of GQDs (a) and the UV spectrum of C-dots (b); (B) Fluorescence spectra of the FRET system: a. GQDs; b. C-dots; c. GQDs/C-dots; (C) Effect of the GQDs concentration on the FRET system.
The quenching effect of imidacloprid on the fluorescence intensity (A) and UV absorption intensity (B) of the FRET systems a–d at imidacloprid concentrations of (0, 5, 500, 1500) × 10−9 mol/L.
(A) Fluorescence response of the FRET system in the presence of imidacloprid a–l (0, 5, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 3000, 4000 × 10−9 mol/L). (B) Calibration curve.
The sample test result and recovery experiments with calibration method.

10–9 mol L–1 (this method, n = 5, α = 0.05)

head.ce:title: Highly selective fluorescence probe for imidacloprid measurement based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Engineering, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Engineering, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, Hainan Provincial Key Laboratory of Quality and Safety for Tropical Fruits and Vegetables, Haikou 571101, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences
Hainan Provincial Key Laboratory of Quality and Safety for Tropical Fruits and Vegetables

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, Hainan Provincial Key Laboratory of Quality and Safety for Tropical Fruits and Vegetables, Haikou 571101, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of GQDs/C-dots system for imidacloprid detection by FRET.

Herein, a fluorescent probe was investigated for the detection of imidacloprid based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between two carbon-based quantum dots, namely graphene- and carbon quantum dots (GQDs/C-dots). In an HAc-NaAc buffer solution at pH = 5.0, an effective FRET occurred between the GQDs and C-dots, significantly enhancing the C-dot fluorescence. When imidacloprid was added to the FRET system, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots was quenched in a linear imidacloprid concentration-dependent manner, which allowed for the establishment of a new method for the determination of imidacloprid by GQD/C-dot FRET. Under optimised experimental conditions, the linear range of imidacloprid was 5–4000 × 10−9 mol L−1, with a correlation coefficient r and a detection limit of 0.998 and 8.23 × 10−10 mol L−1, respectively. This method was applied to the detection of imidacloprid residues in real samples, and a satisfactory recovery rate of standard additions was found between 93.0% and 105.6%.

ce:section.ce:para: Imidacloprid [1] is a new nicotinoid pesticide characterised by high efficiency and a wide action spectrum, and it is widely used in tomatoes, peppers, bananas, and many other crops. While imidacloprid has been identified as a moderately toxic and relatively safe insecticide, some studies have shown that its residues in the agricultural environment and produce are harmful to human health and its toxicity may be underestimated [2]. Therefore, it is necessary to monitor imidacloprid residues in agricultural products and production areas. At present, the common detection methods of imidacloprid include high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [3,4], electrochemical methods [5], and colorimetry methods [6]. HPLC-MS is highly accurate, but it requires expensive equipment and extensive pre-processing. The stability of the electrochemical methods and the sensitivity of the colorimetry methods are generally poor. Therefore, the development of highly selective, highly sensitive, and convenient methods for imidacloprid detection is essential.

Fluorescence analysis is a method for substance identification and quantification based on the shape and intensity of the fluorescence emission spectrum of a compound [7,8].This technique is characterised by high sensitivity and selectivity and is widely used in agricultural and veterinary drug analysis. Therefore, this fluorescence method can also applied to imidacloprid detection [9,10]. However, the existing imidacloprid fluorescence detection assay needs further improvement in terms of sensitivity. The fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET) [11] is a phenomenon occcurs between two fluorescent molecules that are close together. Briefly, the emission spectrum of the donor overlaps the absorption spectrum of the receptor; if the two molecules are within 10 nm from each other, the donor’s fluorescence intensity is quenched, while that of the receptor is enhanced (sensitised fluorescence) [12]. Therefore, an FRET system can significantly enhance the detection sensitivity by quenching the fluorescence intensity of the acceptor, and it can be employed in the analysis of residual molecules of pesticides and veterinary drugs. Quantum dots (QDs) have received increasing attention as a new type of FRET reagent compared with to organic fluorescent molecules used in traditional FRET. Quantum dots (QDs) [13,14] are small semiconductor particles several nanometers in size with unique optical and electronic properties. When a QD is illuminated by UV light, some electrons in it receive enough energy to escape from the atoms and emit strong fluorescence. QDs have many advantages that organic fluorescent molecules do not possess, such as tunable and narrow excitation wavelength and excitability away from the excitation peak [15]. Therefore, QDs can be better FRET donors or acceptors than organic fluorescent molecules. Common QDs include metal QDs (such as CdTe and ZnS) [16,17] and carbon-based ones, namely graphene (GQDs) and carbon (C-dots) QDs [18,19]. At present, metal QDs are widely used in FRET systems [20]; however, their toxicity is relatively high, and the fluorescence yields need improvement. Carbon-based QDs [21] have overcome these toxicity issues, exhibit better biocompatibility, are easy to modify, and have attracted substantial attention in fluorescence analysis in recent years. It has been reported that GQDs, C-dots, and other fluorescent substances can be used to construct FRET systems with good performances [22,23]. Studies have shown that QDs can also act as FRET acceptors. This provides a theoretical basis for the simultaneous use of different QDs as FRET donors and acceptors to establish a new type of FRET system. However, there are no reports on the construction of highly efficient FRET systems using two carbon-based QDs (GQDs and C-dots).

In this study, GQDs and C-dots were synthesised by a hydrothermal method. Subsequently, GQDs were used as energy donors, and C-dots were used as energy acceptors to construct the first GQD/C-dot FRET system. Upon excitation at 380 nm, the GQDs transfer energy to the C-dots, whose fluorescence intensity is effectively amplified. The target molecule imidacloprid can effectively quench the fluorescence emission of C-dots in the FRET system, thereby providing a fluorescence analysis method for imidacloprid detection, as shown in Fig. 1 .

Imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, dinotefuran, acetamiprid, thiacloprid, decidamide, chlorothiacloprid, piperidine, flonicamid and sulfoxaflor standards were purchased from the Shanghai Pesticide Institute. Sodium citrate, sodium hydroxide, concentrated hydrochloric acid, sodium borohydride, ammonium bicarbonate, and Tween-20 were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The reagents used in the experiments were pure, and the water used was deionised water.

The fluorescence measurements were carried out on a F-7000 Fluorescent Spectrometer (HITACHI, Japan). The ultraviolet absorption measurements were carried out on a TU-1901 Dual-beam UV Spectrometer (Beijing General Instrument Co., Ltd, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired with a JEM-2000EX microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of water-soluble carbon-based quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were prepared according to a modified literature method [24]. Briefly, sodium citrate (0.50 g) was weighed in a clean crucible and placed in a constant-temperature drying oven. The temperature was raised to 250 °C, and the compound was reacted for 30 min. After the reaction was completed, the crucible was cooled to 25℃; 10 mL of a sodium hydroxide solution (2.0 mol L−1) was added to the crucible, and its contents were stirred evenly. Subsequently, the solution turned yellow. Then, the mixture was filtered with a high-speed qualitative filter paper. Dilute hydrochloric acid (0.10 mol L−1) was added to adjust the pH of the system to neutral, and the GQDs were obtained.

C-dots were prepared according to a modified hydrothermal method described in the literature [25]. Briefly, sodium citrate (0.40 g), ammonium bicarbonate (3.2 g), and 25 mL of deionised water were added to an autoclave, and the reaction mixture was heated at 300 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to 25℃. Finally, the solution was dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 4 h to obtain the C-dots solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the FRET between GQDs and C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of the two types of QDs were acquired separately. After noting the degree of overlap between the acceptor’s absorption spectrum and the donor’s fluorescence emission spectrum, we acquired the fluorescence emission spectra of the GQDs, C-dots, and GQDs/C-dots mixture to assess the FRET occurring between the two. To further study the FRET phenomenon, we fixed the concentration of C-dots and gradually increased the concentration of the GQDs. Then, we observed the change in the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on determination of imidacloprid by GQDs/C-dots energy transfer system
ce:section.ce:para: In a series of 5 mL colorimetric tubes, 0.1 mL GQDs, 0.2 mL C-dots, imidacloprid at different concentrations, and 20 μL Tween-20 were added in order, diluted to volume with an HAc-NaAc buffer solution at pH = 5.0, and shaken well. After standing and reacting for 5 min, the relative fluorescence intensity of the system was measured upon excitation at λex = 390 nm and setting both the excitation and emission slits at a 5 nm width.

To a 25 g plant-sourced sample, we added 50 mL acetonitrile for 3-min high-speed homogenisation. The mixture was filtered with medium-speed qualitative filter paper. Then, 5 g NaCl was added, and the sample was shaken well. After standing for 30 min, 10 mL of the supernatant was removed and vacuum-dried on a rotary evaporator at 45 °C. Finally, the product was dissolved in 5 mL of an HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 5.0). In a 5 mL colorimetric tube, 0.1 mL GQDs, 0.2 mL C-dots, and 1 mL sample solution were added, diluted to volume with the HAc-NaAc buffer, and shaken well. After reacting for 5 min, the fluorescence intensity was measured.

Water-soluble carbon-based quantum dots were characterized by TEM and a particle size distribution instrument. The results are shown in Fig. 2 . GQDs were spherical in shape, and the distribution was relatively uniform without obvious agglomeration (Fig. 2A). The particle sizes of most particles were between 20 and 60 nm (Fig. 2B). GQDs emitted blue-green fluorescence under ultraviolet light (Fig. 2C). Moreover, the C-dots were elliptical in shape and exhibited a uniform distribution without agglomeration (Fig. 2D), and the particle size of most particles were between 20 and 80 nm (Fig. 2E). The C-dots emitted yellow fluorescence under an ultraviolet light (Fig. 2F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the GQDs/C-dots energy transfer system
ce:section.ce:para: A certain degree of spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor is one of the necessary requirements for an energy transfer between molecules. The fluorescence emission spectrum of the GQDs and the absorption spectrum of the C-dots are shown in Fig. 3 . The emission maximum of the GQDs is at approximately 509 nm, while the absorption maximum of the C-dots is observed at approximately 515 nm; therefore, they present a good overlap (Fig. 3A). Therefore, the GQDs act as energy donors and the C-dots as acceptors. To ensure an efficient energy transfer, an excitation wavelength of 390 nm was selected, which ensured a large absorption from the C-dots while avoiding the simultaneous direct excitation of the GQDs. As shown in Fig. 3B, the emission maximum of the GQDs was near 509 nm, and that of the C-dots was near 589 nm; after mixing the two, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs decreased, while that of the C-dots increased significantly. This confirms that the GQDs transferred energy to the C-dots. To further study the energy transfer system, the concentration of C-dots was fixed while that of GQDs was gradually increased, and the fluorescence spectra of the system were acquired. As shown in Fig. 3C, as the concentration of donor GQDs increased (curve a to curve d), an increasing amount of energy was transferred to the C-dots, and the fluorescence intensity of the latter increased gradually. This further proved that the GQDs/C-dots underwent an effective energy transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the fluorescence parameters of the GQDs/C-dots energy transfer system
ce:section.ce:para: The quantum yield and energy transfer efficiency of fluorescent materials are important parameters of a FRET system. Therefore, these parameters were investigated [26–28]. The quantum yields of the GQDs and C-dots measured by this method were 0.37 and 0.65, respectively. And thenergy transfer efficiency was 0.46 (S1 in Supporting Materials) This result confirms that the GQDs/C-dots system possess a high FRET efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the quenching mechanism of imidacloprid and the fluorescence of the GQDs/C-dots system
ce:section.ce:para: With the addition of imidacloprid, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots in the system continued to decrease, as shown in Fig. 4 A. The quenching mechanism of imidacloprid on C-dots was investigated. Therefore, the mechanism behind the fluorescence quenching of the of C-dots by imidacloprid was studied. Analytes generally affect the fluorescence of QDs through electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonds, van der Waals interactions, or stereoconformational changes at the binding sites. The intrinsic lifetime τ0 was 24.34 ns; in the presence of 50 × 10-9, 100 × 10-9, 500 × 10-9, and 2000 × 10-9 mol L−1 imidacloprid, the fluorescence lifetimes of C-dots were 24.32, 24.10, 23.98, and 23.65 ns, respectively. At concentrations of pyloprid ranging from 0 to 2 × 10−9 mol L−1, the fluorescence lifetime of C-dots decreased only by 0.67 ns, indicating that the QD lifetime was almost unaffected in the imidacloprid concentration range employed in this study; therefore, the C-dots fluorescence quenching in the present system is mainly a static process. The interaction between GQDs/C-dots and imidacloprid was further studied using UV–vis spectroscopy. Fig. 4B presents the absorption spectra of the GQDs/C-dot solution in the presence of different concentrations ofimidacloprid. As the concentration of imidacloprid increases, the absorbance value of the GQDs/C-dots gradually decreases from the initial value of 0.254 to 0.224, corresponding to a distinct reduction in the solution’s colour intensity. These results prove that GQDs/C-dots andimidaclopridmolecules form a new molecular system via static interactions, which is consistent with the results of the lifetime measurements. We can hypothesise thatimidacloprid, which presents a positive charge in an acidic solution, is engaged in an electrostatic attraction with the negatively charged surface of the C-dots.

The effects of the buffer’s pH, amount of surfactant, and quenching reaction time on the detection of imidacloprid were investigated. As shown in Fig.S1A, when HAc-NaAc (pH = 5.0) was selected as the buffer, the fluorescence quenching (fluorescence quenching intensity values, △I) in the system was optimal. The surfactant Tween-20 formed micelles in the system and reduced the distance between the molecules. When the dosage of Tween-20 was 30 µL, the fluorescence quenching effect was the highest (Fig.S1B). The effects of different reaction times on the fluorescence intensity of the system were also investigated. When the reaction time reached 3 min, the quenching effect was maximum and remained unchanged (Fig.S1C). Therefore, the optimal conditions for the experiments were the use of HAc-NaAc buffer at pH = 5.0, a dosage of Tween-20 of 30 µL, and a reaction time of 3 min.

Selectivity is an important indicator of the performance of a detection method. To investigate the ability of this system to selectively recognise imidacloprid, we analysed the possible interference from other neonicotinoid pesticides as well as pesticides with molecular structure ssimilar to that ofimidacloprid. According to the experimental method, we measured the fluorescence intensity of 5 × 10−8 mol L−1 imidacloprid and those of mixed systems containing 5 × 10−8 mol/L imidacloprid with 2.5 × 10−7 mol L−1 thiamethoxam, dinotefuran, acetamiprid, thiacloprid, decidamide, chlorothiacloprid, piperidine, flonicamid, sulfoxaflor and all of the above distractors, respectively. The results are presented in Fig.S2. After the addition of the interfering compounds, the fluorescence intensity of the systems was nearly unchanged (the relative difference, RD % is less than 5%). The above results indicate that the present energy transfer system presents good selectivity for the recognition of imidacloprid.

The standard curve was obtained under the optimal experimental conditions, as shown in Fig. 5 . The results showed that the concentration of imidacloprid in the range of 5 – 4000 × 10−9 mol L−1 was in a linear relationship with the emission intensity ratio at 589 nm (I0/I). The linear regression equation used was I0 /I = -0.0002c (10−9 mol L−1) + 0.9886. A correlation coefficient r of 0.998 and a detection limit of 8.23 × 10−10 mol L−1 (DL = KSb/a, K = 3) were used. This method exhibited greater sensitivity and a lower detection limit than other imidacloprid detection methods reported in the literature (Table S2).

Real samples were tested according to the developed experimental method; the standard addition and recovery tests were carried out simultaneously. The results (Table 1 ) of these tests were found to be consistent with those obtained using a conventional HPLC-MS analysis. The recovery rate of the method is ranged from 86.2% to 110.7%.

Two carbon-based QDs, namely GQDs and C-dots, characterised by a high fluorescence quantum yield, were employed in the development of a new FRET system. The higher energy transfer efficiency significantly enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the receptor, thereby improving the sensitivity of the system in imidacloprid detection. This FRET system, with its simple operation and low cost, provides a new method for the rapid detection of pesticide residues.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This manuscript was supported by China Agriculture Research System of MOF and MARA (CARS-31) and the Central Public-interest Scientific Institution Basal Research Fund for Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences (1630082017002 and 1630082017003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.107172.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
